paper_title: Continual Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Semantic Segmentation

problem_definition.motivation: Pixel-level labeling for real-world images is costly in time and labor. A domain discrepancy exists between synthetic images (which are easier to label) and real-world images.
problem_definition.limitation_of_existing_methods: Existing Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) methods are limited because they typically focus on a single-target setting, which is impractical for real-world scenarios with multiple, sequentially introduced target domains. Applying these methods to a continual UDA setting results in catastrophic forgetting of previously learned domains.

proposed_method.name: Expanding Target-specific Memory (ETM) framework
proposed_method.core_idea: To overcome catastrophic forgetting in a Continual UDA setting, the framework introduces a dedicated memory for each target domain. When testing on a previous domain, the corresponding stored memory is used to restore performance.

key_components.name: Target-specific Memory (TM)
key_components.description: A lightweight sub-memory that is initiated, trained, and stored for each target domain. It is designed to hold unique information corresponding to each domain's specific discrepancy.

key_components.name: Double Hinge Adversarial (DHA) loss
key_components.description: A loss function designed to enhance the performance of the framework.

key_findings.main_result: The ETM framework successfully alleviates catastrophic forgetting for previously learned target domains when adapting to new ones sequentially.
key_findings.qualitative_evidence: Qualitative results (Fig. 1(c)) demonstrate that the proposed framework mitigates forgetting on the previous target domain compared to the baseline UDA method.
key_findings.comparison_with_related_work: The style transfer method from Wu et al. [38] was found to be insufficient for overcoming catastrophic forgetting on multiple real-world datasets, as style transfer alone could not handle considerable domain discrepancies.

title: Continual Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Semantic Segmentation
